reasoning: Output A wins on DDD correctness and explicit pattern adherence. Its most critical advantage is domain events: A raises OrderCreatedEvent and OrderConfirmedEvent (expectation 10 fully met), while B raises no domain events at all. A also uses a proper ValueObject base class with structural equality via GetEqualityComponents(), whereas B uses `sealed record` which gives value equality for free but lacks a shared ValueObject abstraction and explicit equality contract. Both outputs satisfy most business rules, but A's architecture is more canonically DDD (AggregateRoot<TId>, Entity<TId>, ValueObject hierarchy, IDomainEvent), more closely matching the eval task's implicit DDD framing. B has genuine strengths — its tests are more thorough (more edge cases, encapsulation checks, accumulation loop tests), its Money type rounds to 2 decimal places, it has a Multiply operation, and its Currency is a type-safe enum rather than a raw string. However, the total missing of domain events in B is a decisive failure on expectation 10, and B's `Result<T>` is generic-only (no non-generic `Result` type for void operations), which means `Confirm()` returns `Result<Order>` returning `this` — awkward and semantically off. B also forces at least one line item at Order creation rather than at Confirm, which misreads the business rule ('min 1 item enforced at confirmation' vs. 'at creation'). A correctly enforces the min-item rule at Confirm(), not Create(), which is the more natural and flexible DDD design. Rubric scores below reflect these findings.

strengths: Full DDD hierarchy: AggregateRoot<TId>, Entity<TId>, ValueObject base class with GetEqualityComponents(), IDomainEvent interface
Domain events raised: OrderCreatedEvent on Create(), OrderConfirmedEvent on Confirm() — satisfies expectation 10
Private constructors + static factory methods returning Result<T> on all domain types (Order, OrderLineItem, Money, Quantity)
Private setters on all entity properties (Order, OrderLineItem)
Both Result (non-generic, void) and Result<T> present — Confirm() correctly returns Result (not Result<Order>)
Min 1 item rule enforced at Confirm() — correct design allows creating empty orders and adding items before confirming
$10K max enforced at AddLineItem() with exact boundary test (exactly 10000 allowed)
No-adds-after-confirm enforced and tested
Tests cover all 5 business rules with boundary cases (exactly $10K, quantity-based overflow)
Tests are behavioral: check outcomes, error messages, and domain event identity
Separate ValueObjects/ folder with proper namespace organization
EF Core-friendly: parameterless private constructors present
weaknesses: TotalAmount computed by iterating lineItems directly rather than calling LineTotal — bypasses Money.Add() for the check in AddLineItem() (uses raw decimal arithmetic for the guard, then Money.Add() for the property), a minor inconsistency
Currency represented as raw string — no type-safety (though validation is present)
Tests missing GlobalUsings.cs that makes `using OrderAggregate.Domain` implicit — minor, not a correctness issue

strengths: Type-safe Currency enum (USD, EUR, GBP, DKK) — better than raw string
Money.Create() rounds to 2 decimal places — more realistic monetary arithmetic
Money.Multiply() method cleanly handles quantity multiplication with Result<T>
Currency mismatch between order and line items is explicitly enforced
Sealed classes used throughout — correct for value-object-like entities
Tests are very thorough: encapsulation checks (ReadOnly cast test), accumulation loop (100 items), multi-currency, edge cases
More test cases overall (~30 vs ~20) with region organization for readability
Result<T>.Match() method provided for functional composition
weaknesses: No domain events raised anywhere — OrderCreatedEvent and OrderConfirmedEvent are completely absent (expectation 10 FAIL)
No IDomainEvent interface, no AggregateRoot class — missing foundational DDD infrastructure
Money and Quantity use `sealed record` — record value equality works but is incidental, not an explicit ValueObject contract; no shared ValueObject base class
No non-generic Result type: Confirm() returns Result<Order> and returns `this`, which is semantically misleading (callers might use the returned Order but it's the same instance)

Order.Create() requires a line item at construction — semantically wrong per the task ('min 1 item' is an invariant enforced at confirmation, not at creation). This makes it impossible to create an empty draft order and add items iteratively in the most natural way
No EF Core-friendly parameterless constructors on any class — cannot be used with ORM without modification

Quantity record value equality relies on record's positional equality, but Quantity has a private constructor so the synthesized Equals may not work as expected with positional properties — needs verification
Result<T> only, no void Result — forces all void-semantics operations to return Result<SomeType>

details.text: Value objects (Money, Quantity) are separate classes with value equality, not raw primitives
details.note: Money and Quantity extend ValueObject base class with GetEqualityComponents(). Tests verify equality.

details.text: Domain entities have private setters — no public property setters on Order or OrderLineItem
details.note: All properties on Order (CustomerId, Status, LineItems) and OrderLineItem (ProductId, Quantity, UnitPrice) have private or no setters.

details.text: Entities use private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>
details.note: Order.Create(), OrderLineItem.Create(), Money.Create(), Quantity.Create() all use private constructors and return Result<T>.

details.text: Result<T> pattern is used for operations that can fail — no throwing exceptions for business rule violations
details.note: All business rule violations return Result.Failure(). No exceptions thrown for domain rules.

details.text: Order aggregate enforces the $10,000 maximum total invariant
details.note: MaxOrderValue = 10_000m constant. Checked in AddLineItem(). Boundary test: exactly $10K succeeds, $10K+$0.01 fails.

details.text: Order aggregate prevents adding items after confirmation
details.note: AddLineItem() checks Status == OrderStatus.Confirmed and returns failure. Tested in AddLineItem_WhenOrderConfirmed_ReturnsFailure.

details.text: Order requires at least one line item (enforced at creation or confirmation)
details.note: Confirm() checks _lineItems.Count == 0. Confirm_WithNoItems_ReturnsFailure test confirms this.

details.text: xUnit tests exist covering all 5 business rules
details.note: Tests cover: positive qty (QuantityTests), non-negative price (MoneyTests), min 1 item (Confirm_WithNoItems), $10K max (AddLineItem_WhenTotalWouldExceed10000), no adds after confirm (AddLineItem_WhenOrderConfirmed).

details.text: Tests are behavioral — they test business outcomes, not just getters/setters
details.note: Tests assert failure reasons via error message content, check domain event identity, verify status transitions.

details.text: Domain events are raised for significant state changes (order created, confirmed)
details.note: OrderCreatedEvent raised in Order.Create(). OrderConfirmedEvent raised in Order.Confirm(). Both tested explicitly (Create_RaisesOrderCreatedEvent, Confirm_RaisesOrderConfirmedEvent).

details.text: Value objects (Money, Quantity) are separate classes with value equality, not raw primitives
details.note: Money and Quantity are sealed records in separate files with value equality. Tests verify equality. No explicit ValueObject base class but record equality works correctly.

details.text: Domain entities have private setters — no public property setters on Order or OrderLineItem
details.note: Order.Status is private set; Currency, Id, LineItems are get-only. OrderLineItem all get-only. Fully encapsulated.

details.text: Entities use private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>
details.note: Order.Create(), OrderLineItem.Create(), Money.Create(), Quantity.Create() all use private constructors and return Result<T>.

details.text: Result<T> pattern is used for operations that can fail — no throwing exceptions for business rule violations
details.note: All business rule violations return Result<T>.Failure(). No exceptions thrown for domain rules.

details.text: Order aggregate enforces the $10,000 maximum total invariant
details.note: MaxOrderValue = 10_000m. Enforced in AddLineItemInternal(). Boundary tested (exactly $10K succeeds, $10,001 fails).

details.text: Order aggregate prevents adding items after confirmation
details.note: AddLineItem() checks Status == Confirmed and returns failure. AddLineItem_ToConfirmedOrder_ReturnsFailure test confirms.

details.text: Order requires at least one line item (enforced at creation or confirmation)
details.note: Enforced at Order.Create() (requires first line item). Confirm() also checks _lineItems.Count == 0. Partial: design forces item at create which differs from canonical 'enforced at confirm' pattern, but the invariant is technically enforced.

details.text: xUnit tests exist covering all 5 business rules
details.note: Tests cover: positive qty, non-negative price, min 1 item (implicit via Create requiring item), $10K max, no adds after confirm.

details.text: Tests are behavioral — they test business outcomes, not just getters/setters
details.note: Tests check error messages, status transitions, accumulation correctness, encapsulation (ReadOnly cast check). Behavioral quality is strong.

details.text: Domain events are raised for significant state changes (order created, confirmed)
details.note: No domain events exist. No IDomainEvent interface, no AggregateRoot with DomainEvents collection, no OrderCreatedEvent or OrderConfirmedEvent. This expectation is completely unmet.
